title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a woman called Eva talking about a language café project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a woman called Eva talking about a language café project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A woman called Eva talks about a conversation project for language learners.
extracts.audio_script: Hi. I’m Eva and I organise Willowbrook Language Café, a weekly meeting where people practise English in a friendly, informal way. It began after several residents said formal evening classes were not enough: they needed opportunities to speak naturally. Our first meetings took place in a café, but it became too noisy on busy evenings. We now meet in the [9]community library[/9], which stays open late once a week and gives us a quiet room with tables, comfortable chairs and access for wheelchair users.||The café welcomes adults at different levels. Nobody is tested on arrival, and people may listen at first if they feel nervous. Volunteers lead small group conversations rather than teaching grammar lessons. The topic people ask to practise most often is [10]job interviews[/10], because several learners are confident in daily conversation but become worried when answering formal questions. New conversation volunteers attend a brief introduction. It includes advice about speaking clearly, but the central point is how to give [11]positive feedback[/11] without correcting every small mistake and interrupting the conversation.||Meetings begin with short activities. Volunteers used to photocopy a long list of questions every week, but much of the paper was wasted. Each table now receives a reusable pack of [12]conversation cards[/12] with pictures and questions on different themes. People may move between tables during the evening. We provide tea, coffee and biscuits free of charge. Participants who prefer another drink are encouraged to bring a [13]water bottle[/13], especially in the summer when the room can become warm. The meetings are relaxed, but we ask everybody to arrive on time so groups can begin together.||Besides ordinary conversation sessions, we organise occasional trips. Last term we went to the town museum, which gave learners useful practice asking questions. This term, the most popular suggested outing was a visit to the [14]farmers' market[/14], where people can talk to stallholders about local food. Anyone joining that visit will be paired with a volunteer for support. We have also developed resources for people unable to attend weekly meetings: our website now offers a collection of short [15]listening activities[/15], recorded by volunteers speaking about everyday experiences. Learners can hear different accents and answer simple questions afterwards.||Volunteers gain useful experience too. Those attending at least eight meetings can take part in a free [16]teaching workshop[/16], led by an experienced language tutor. At the end of each year, we celebrate what everyone has achieved. In previous years people brought food from their home countries; this time learners voted to prepare an international [17]music evening[/17], where songs and short explanations will be shared. We do not charge people to attend the café, so we need community support. Money raised during that event will buy new [18]children's books[/18] for families who use the library and are learning English together. If you would like to volunteer, please collect an application form after this talk.

dialogue.speaker: eva
dialogue.text: Hi. I’m Eva and I organise Willowbrook Language Café, a weekly meeting where people practise English in a friendly, informal way.
